notes: Check for anonymous login; look for writable dirs and config leaks.
Grab the banner for version. Brute force only with a user list; watch rate-limits.
Cleartext. Try default creds; sniff traffic if you can MITM.
User enumeration via VRFY/EXPN/RCPT. Check for open relay.
Attempt a zone transfer (AXFR); enumerate subdomains.
Map the app, fuzz content with ffuf, run nikto, check headers and robots.txt.
Domain controller signal. Try AS-REP roasting and user enumeration.
Mail retrieval. Default creds and cleartext auth worth a look.
Enumerate RPC services; often exposes NFS exports on 2049.
Windows endpoint mapper. Enumerate with rpcdump / impacket.
Legacy SMB session service. Enumerate shares and null sessions.
Mail access. Banner-grab and test default credentials.
Try community strings (public/private); walk for system + user data.
AD enumeration goldmine. Anonymous bind, dump users/groups.
As 80, plus inspect the TLS cert for hostnames and internal names.
List shares, null session, check for EternalBlue and signing.
List modules anonymously (rsync rsync://<TARGET>/); often world-readable. Pull or push files, even to home dirs.
Try sa / weak creds; xp_cmdshell for RCE if you get in.
showmount -e to list exports; mount and look for no_root_squash.
Test root / blank password; read files via LOAD_FILE if privileged.
Check NLA; spray known creds carefully (lockout risk).
Try postgres / weak creds; COPY ... TO PROGRAM for RCE.
Remote PowerShell. With valid creds, evil-winrm is your shell.
Often unauthenticated; try redis-cli, check for RCE via module load or webshell write.
Common for proxies and app servers — Tomcat manager, Jenkins, etc.
Frequently open with no auth; query indices for sensitive data.
Often no auth on older installs; connect and dump collections.
